lesson.menu_desc: Share your opinion with confidence and tact — opinion phrases, hedging language and softeners.
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 — Expressing Opinions Professionally
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Daniela Feitoza | Aula 13 -- Expressing Opinions Professionally

listenings.text: Hi Daniela, it is Sarah, your English coach. Today, listen to one short voice note about giving opinions at work. In a professional meeting, how you say your opinion matters as much as the opinion itself. Notice three things. First, I open with a clear phrase. In my view, the new tool will save us time. Second, when I am not completely sure, I soften the idea. It seems to me that the rollout might take a little longer than planned. Words like might, perhaps, and a little are softeners. They make me sound careful, not weak. Third, when I disagree, I stay polite. I see your point, but I think we should test it first. I never say you are wrong. Try this in your next meeting. State your view, soften it when needed, and disagree with respect. Talk soon.

Hello Daniela, this is Marcus. Thanks for sharing your opinion in the meeting today. I want to give you some honest feedback, the kind a colleague gives. Overall, you did well. You opened with a strong phrase. From my perspective, we should adopt the new platform. That was clear and confident. One small thing. When the team disagreed, you said they were wrong. I would soften that a little. Try something like, I see your point, but I am not sure that is the best option. It sounds more open, and people listen more. Also, when you are not certain, it is fine to hedge. Say, it might work, or perhaps we could try a pilot first. That is not weak. It is professional. Keep stating your views. Just add a little softness, and you will sound like a senior manager. Talk soon.
